slides.title: Every Machine Is a Door
slides.subtitle: Device Vulnerabilities: the categories of computing devices, the malware adversaries deploy, the weaknesses they exploit, and how to assess and document the resulting risk
slides.footerLeft: AP Cybersecurity · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2026)
slides.script: Welcome to Unit 4, Securing Devices, and Lesson 4.1, Device Vulnerabilities. This single comprehensive deck covers the whole topic: four learning objectives that form the threat-side foundation for everything else in the unit. We start by naming the kinds of computing devices an organization runs, then the malware adversaries use as tools, then the common weaknesses they exploit, and finally how to assess and document the risk each weakness creates. Our anchor is a connected farm: every server, laptop, tablet, and embedded controller out in those fields is a potential door for an adversary. The end-of-lesson quiz and the teacher guide live on this file and span all four learning objectives, A through D.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Identify the five types of computing devices — server, personal, handheld, embedded, and IoT — by what each one is for (LO 4.1.A)
Name the major types of malware — viruses, worms, trojans and RATs, ransomware, spyware, keyloggers, logic bombs, rootkits, and fileless malware — by their defining behavior (LO 4.1.B)

Explain how adversaries exploit common device vulnerabilities — unpatched software, weak authentication, missing BIOS/UEFI passwords, autorun, open ports, missing firewalls, and missing anti-malware — to cause loss, damage, disruption, or destruction (LO 4.1.C)
Assess and document the risk from a device vulnerability as high, moderate, or low based on the device's criticality and the impact of exploitation (LO 4.1.D)

slides.script: Four objectives today, one for each learning objective in the topic. First, identify the five categories of computing devices by what each is for, not just by size. Second, name the major types of malware by the one behavior that defines each. Third, explain how adversaries exploit the common device vulnerabilities the CED lists, from unpatched software to a missing firewall. Fourth, take a vulnerability and rate the risk as high, moderate, or low, then document it. By the end you can look at any device on that connected farm, name its category, predict the malware and weaknesses it faces, and assign a defensible risk level.

slides.prompt: A modern farm runs on computers you might not think of as computers: a back-office machine that hands out network addresses to everything else, the staff's laptops, a worker's smartphone and smart watch out in the field, and a low-cost controller built into a tractor that connects to the internet to report its location.
slides.subprompt: Write 3–5 sentences: which of these five things are 'computers'? Group them however makes sense to you, and explain what makes the tractor's controller different from the office laptop. Don't worry about official terms yet — just describe what each device is for.
slides.directions: Independent writing. I will cold-call 2–3 students.
slides.script: Two minutes, on your own. Read the list: a machine that hands out addresses to the whole network, the staff laptops, a worker's phone and watch in the field, and a little controller built into a tractor that phones home over the internet. In three to five sentences, decide which of these count as computers and group them however feels natural. Tell me what makes that tractor controller different from an office laptop. Don't reach for official terms yet — by the end of objective A you'll have the College Board's five categories, and every device on this list will land in exactly one of them.

slides.heading: What You'll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Guided Notes have four sections that match today's four objectives. Green = CB required. Blue = enrichment.

sections.title: Five Types of Computing Devices
sections.description: Server, personal, handheld, embedded, and IoT — defined by purpose, with the CED examples for each (EK 4.1.A.1–A.5).

sections.title: The Malware Toolkit
sections.description: Viruses, worms, trojans/RATs, ransomware, spyware, keyloggers, logic bombs, rootkits, and fileless malware — each one's defining behavior (EK 4.1.B.1–B.3).

sections.title: Exploiting Common Device Weaknesses
sections.description: Unpatched software, weak authentication, missing BIOS/UEFI passwords, autorun, open ports, missing firewalls, and missing anti-malware (EK 4.1.C.1–C.7).

sections.title: Assessing and Documenting Risk
sections.description: Rate device-vulnerability risk as high, moderate, or low based on criticality and impact, and record it (EK 4.1.D.1–D.4).

sections.title: Enrichment: Fileless Malware
sections.description: Why malware that lives in RAM and rides legitimate programs defeats a simple drive scan (EK 4.1.B.3).

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. The Lesson 4.1 page on apcsexamprep.com has every answer if you fall behind.
slides.script: Four green sections plus one blue enrichment. Section one names the five device categories by purpose. Section two is the malware toolkit — nine named types, each with the one behavior that defines it. Section three is how adversaries exploit seven common weaknesses. Section four is risk: rating each vulnerability high, moderate, or low and writing it down. The enrichment digs into fileless malware, the type that doesn't sit on the drive at all. Keep your pencil moving; I collect Guided Notes at the end of class.

slides.label: Five Types of Computing Devices
slides.script: Section one. Learning objective 4.1.A. Before we can talk about how devices get attacked, we have to name the kinds of devices. The College Board gives five categories, and the trick is that each is defined by what the device is for, not by how big or expensive it is. Ten minutes.

slides.heading: Server, Personal, and Handheld — Defined by Purpose
bullets: Server computer — provides one or more services to OTHER computers, such as DNS, DHCP, or FTP. Any computer can be a server; in an enterprise it usually has more processing power and storage than a personal computer (EK 4.1.A.1).
Personal computer — designed to be used by one person for work or recreation: word processing, graphic design, web browsing, media. Includes desktops, laptops, and notebooks (EK 4.1.A.2).
Handheld computer (mobile computer / information appliance) — smaller than a personal computer and runs on battery power: tablets, smartphones, and wearables like smart watches (EK 4.1.A.3).
The test is purpose, not size: a machine becomes a server because it serves other computers, not because of its hardware.
slides.script: Three of the five categories. A server provides services to other computers — DNS, DHCP, FTP — and the key line from EK 4.1.A.1 is that any computer can be a server. In an enterprise it tends to have more power and storage, but that's a tendency, not the definition. A personal computer is built for one person doing work or recreation: desktops, laptops, notebooks, EK 4.1.A.2. A handheld is smaller than a personal computer and battery-powered — tablets, smartphones, smart watches, EK 4.1.A.3. Notice the throughline: every category is defined by what the device is for. On the farm, the address-handing-out machine is a server because of what it does, even if it's the same box as a laptop.

slides.heading: Embedded and IoT: The Computers Inside the Machines
slides.context: Embedded computers are PART of a machine (EK 4.1.A.4); when those everyday devices connect to the internet, they're called IoT devices (EK 4.1.A.5).
rows: Embedded (EK 4.1.A.4)
Part of a machine; specialized instruction set for that machine's components; slower, cheaper, minimal storage
The controller built into a tractor, car, train, or airplane
IoT (EK 4.1.A.5)
An everyday device with an embedded computer that is internet-connected
Washing machines, coffee makers, thermostats; connected farm equipment
Critical infrastructure (EK 4.1.A.5)
Embedded computers operating infrastructure
Circuit breakers at electrical substations; pumps at water treatment plants
Medical (EK 4.1.A.5)
Embedded computers in medical equipment
IV pumps, MRI scanners, pacemakers, insulin pumps
slides.note: AP TIP: A device can wear two labels — the tractor's controller is BOTH embedded (part of the machine) AND IoT (internet-connected). Name both when both apply.
slides.script: The last two categories live inside other machines. An embedded computer, EK 4.1.A.4, is part of a machine: it has a specialized instruction set for that machine's components, and it tends to be slower, cheaper, and short on storage. When an everyday device with an embedded computer connects to the internet, EK 4.1.A.5 calls it an IoT device — washing machines, thermostats, and our connected farm equipment. The same EK reaches into critical infrastructure, the breakers and water-plant pumps, and into medical equipment, the IV pumps and pacemakers. The exam tip: a device can carry two labels at once. That tractor controller is both embedded and IoT, so name both.

slides.heading: Any Computer Can Be a Server
slides.misconception: A server is a special class of expensive hardware that is fundamentally different from a personal computer.
slides.correction: A server is a device that PROVIDES one or more services to other computers (DNS, DHCP, FTP). Any computer can be a server. More power and storage is a tendency in enterprises, not what makes it a server (EK 4.1.A.1).
slides.whyItHappens: Students picture a rack of expensive blades in a data center and assume 'server' means the hardware, so they classify by appearance instead of by the role the device plays.
slides.example: On the farm, an ordinary box becomes 'the server' the moment it starts handing out DNS and DHCP to the other computers — same hardware, different role.
slides.script: The most common slip in objective A: thinking 'server' means a particular expensive box. EK 4.1.A.1 is explicit — any computer can be a server, because a server is defined by providing services to other computers. The extra power and storage you see in enterprise servers is a tendency, not the definition. Classify by role, not by looks. On the farm, the moment a plain machine starts serving DNS and DHCP to everything else, it's the server.

slides.label: The Malware Toolkit
slides.script: Section two. Learning objective 4.1.B. Malware is the tool adversaries use to carry out part of their plan. The College Board names nine types, and the exam asks you to match a behavior to a name. We'll take them in two grids, then lock them into vocabulary. Fifteen minutes.

slides.heading: Spreading and Hiding: Virus, Worm, Trojan/RAT, Ransomware
slides.subheading: EK 4.1.B.1–B.2 — malware is malicious software used as a tool; these four are the headline file-based types.

items.title: Virus
items.summary: Malware that must be activated by a user executing or opening a file — it does not spread on its own; a person has to run it.

items.title: Worm
items.summary: Malware that spreads from one computer to another WITHOUT human interaction — no one has to click anything for it to propagate.

items.title: Trojan / RAT
items.summary: Malware embedded in other software that seems harmless; a remote access trojan (RAT) specifically gives an adversary remote access to the target system.

items.title: Ransomware
items.summary: Encrypts a device's files so the user can't access them, then demands payment for a decryption key within a fixed amount of time.

slides.script: First grid, the headline types. EK 4.1.B.1 sets the frame: malware is malicious software, a tool an adversary uses to accomplish part of their plan. Now four types from EK 4.1.B.2. A virus must be activated by a user opening or running a file — a person has to act. A worm spreads computer to computer with no human interaction at all. A trojan hides inside software that looks harmless, and a remote access trojan, a RAT, specifically hands the adversary remote control. Ransomware encrypts your files and demands payment for the decryption key on a deadline. The virus-versus-worm line — does a human have to act — is the single most tested distinction here.

slides.heading: Watching and Triggering: Spyware, Keylogger, Logic Bomb, Rootkit
slides.subheading: EK 4.1.B.2 — four more types built around surveillance, conditions, and deep concealment.

items.title: Spyware
items.summary: Tracks a user's actions on a computer and sends the information back to an adversary.

items.title: Keylogger
items.summary: Software or hardware that logs the user's keystrokes and sends them to the adversary, who can often extract usernames and passwords.

items.title: Logic Bomb
items.summary: Set to trigger its effect only when a specific set of conditions is met — for example, a particular date or event.

items.title: Rootkit
items.summary: Sophisticated malware that gets into the OS and can control nearly every aspect of the system, including making itself invisible to detection.

slides.script: Second grid, four more from EK 4.1.B.2. Spyware tracks what you do and reports back. A keylogger captures keystrokes — hardware or software — and that's how adversaries harvest usernames and passwords. A logic bomb waits: it only triggers when specific conditions are met, like a date or an event, which is why an insider can plant one before leaving. A rootkit burrows into the operating system, controls nearly everything, and crucially makes itself invisible to detection. Notice these four are about watching, waiting, and hiding rather than spreading. Write each name with its one-line behavior.

slides.heading: Enrichment: Fileless Malware Beats the Drive Scan
slides.subheading: EK 4.1.B.3 — most malware is a file, but the exception is the one that hides in plain sight.

left.title: What Makes It Different (EK 4.1.B.3)
left.definition: Lives in RAM, not on disk
examples: Most malware is a file or a collection of files sitting on a drive
Fileless malware is malicious CODE that lives in RAM (EK 4.1.B.3)
It uses legitimate programs ALREADY installed on the device to compromise it
There is no file on the drive for a simple scan to find

right.title: Why It's Hard to Catch
right.definition: Nothing to scan, nothing obviously foreign
examples: Drive scanners look for files; fileless malware leaves none
It rides trusted, installed tools, so it looks like normal activity
Rebooting can clear RAM-only code — but the entry point often remains
Defense shifts from 'scan the disk' to 'watch behavior of trusted programs'

slides.footer: TAKEAWAY: EK 4.1.B.3 is the exception to 'malware is a file.' If a scenario says the threat lives in RAM and uses programs already on the device, it's fileless malware — and that's exactly why a plain drive scan misses it.
slides.script: Quick enrichment on the one type that breaks the pattern. EK 4.1.B.3 says most malware is a file or collection of files — but fileless malware is malicious code that lives in RAM and uses legitimate programs already installed on the device. Read that carefully: there's no file on the drive for a scanner to catch, and it rides trusted tools so it blends in with normal activity. That's why a plain drive scan misses it entirely, and why defense shifts toward watching how trusted programs behave. On the exam, 'lives in RAM, uses installed legitimate programs' is the tell for fileless malware.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary (LO 4.1.B)

terms.definition: Malicious software that can damage or destroy a device or network, or allow an adversary access to a device and its data (EK 4.1.B.1).
Malware that must be activated by a user executing or opening a file (EK 4.1.B.2).
Malware that spreads from one computer to another without human interaction (EK 4.1.B.2).
Malware embedded in seemingly harmless software; a RAT gives an adversary remote access to the target system (EK 4.1.B.2).
Malware that encrypts a device's files and demands payment for a decryption key within a fixed time (EK 4.1.B.2).
Malware that tracks a user's actions and sends the information back to an adversary (EK 4.1.B.2).
Software or hardware that logs keystrokes to extract usernames and passwords (EK 4.1.B.2).
Malware set to trigger its effect only when a specific set of conditions is met (EK 4.1.B.2).
Sophisticated malware that controls the OS and hides itself from detection (EK 4.1.B.2).
Malicious code that lives in RAM and uses legitimate installed programs rather than existing as a file on disk (EK 4.1.B.3).

slides.script: Ten terms for objective B. Malware is the umbrella, EK 4.1.B.1. Then the nine types, each tied to its EK: virus needs a user to run a file; worm spreads on its own; trojan hides in harmless software and a RAT gives remote control; ransomware encrypts and demands payment; spyware watches; keylogger captures keystrokes; logic bomb waits for conditions; rootkit hides in the OS; and fileless malware lives in RAM using installed programs. Memorize these by name and behavior, because the exam gives you a behavior and asks for the name. Get all ten into your notes.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Independent Writing
slides.subheading: Both questions map to LO 4.1.A and 4.1.B. Complete on your Guided Notes packet.
prompts: A connected grain-moisture sensor is a low-cost computer built into the silo, with minimal storage and a specialized instruction set, and it reports readings over the internet. Name the TWO CED categories it fits and cite each EK. Then explain why it fits both.

An employee opens an emailed attachment that looks like an invoice; nothing happens until it's opened, after which it corrupts records on that one machine. Name the malware type and cite its EK. How would your answer change if the same code had spread to other machines with no one clicking anything?
slides.directions: Independent. 6 minutes. Full sentences. I will collect Guided Notes at the end of class.
slides.script: Two questions, six minutes, independent, full sentences. Question one: that grain-moisture sensor is built into the silo and reports over the internet — name both categories it fits and cite each EK. You should land on embedded, EK 4.1.A.4, and IoT, EK 4.1.A.5, and explain why both apply. Question two: the invoice attachment that only acts when opened is a virus, EK 4.1.B.2 — and if the same code spread on its own with no clicking, it would be a worm instead. That virus-versus-worm pivot is exactly how the exam tests it. Go.

slides.label: Exploiting Common Device Weaknesses
slides.script: Section three. Learning objective 4.1.C. Now the weaknesses adversaries exploit to cause loss, damage, disruption, or destruction. The CED lists seven. We'll group them into software-and-access weaknesses and network-and-defense weaknesses, then practice matching a scenario to the exact vulnerability. Fifteen minutes.

slides.heading: Software, Authentication, and Boot Weaknesses
slides.subheading: EK 4.1.C.1–C.4 — four ways in that start with software, passwords, firmware, and removable media.

left.title: Unpatched Software & Weak Authentication
examples: Unpatched software: adversaries develop exploits for known vulnerabilities, letting them crash a system, view user actions, toggle components (webcam/mic), or take control to steal or destroy data (EK 4.1.C.1)
Weak authentication: adversaries guess a password, or use social engineering to get the user to divulge it (EK 4.1.C.2)

right.title: Missing BIOS/UEFI Password & Autorun
examples: No BIOS/UEFI password: an adversary boots into a special mode (e.g., recovery mode) for higher privileges, can load their own OS from an external drive, and alter or create user profiles — including changing passwords (EK 4.1.C.3)
Autorun enabled: malware loaded onto an external drive runs automatically the moment the drive is inserted (EK 4.1.C.4)

slides.footer: AP TIP: These four often need different access. Unpatched software (C.1) and weak auth (C.2) can be remote; a missing BIOS/UEFI password (C.3) and autorun (C.4) usually need physical access to the device.
slides.script: First grid of weaknesses, EK 4.1.C.1 through C.4. Unpatched software, C.1: adversaries write exploits for known holes, and an unpatched device lets them crash it, watch the user, flip a webcam or mic on, or fully take control to steal or destroy data. Weak authentication, C.2: they simply guess the password or socially engineer the user into handing it over. Missing BIOS or UEFI password, C.3: with brief physical access they boot into recovery mode for higher privileges, even load their own operating system from an external drive, and change user profiles and passwords. Autorun, C.4: malware on an inserted external drive runs automatically. Notice C.1 and C.2 can be remote, while C.3 and C.4 usually need hands on the device.

slides.heading: Network and Defense Weaknesses: Open Ports, No Firewall, No Anti-Malware
slides.subheading: EK 4.1.C.5–C.7 — three weaknesses about reaching the device and failing to filter or detect what arrives.

items.title: Open Ports
items.summary: Adversaries can leverage open ports to connect to a device — an open port is a way in over the network.

items.title: Missing / Misconfigured Firewall
items.summary: Adversaries send malicious data to disrupt or take control of a device; a device with no firewall (or a misconfigured one) cannot filter out that malicious data.

items.title: Missing Anti-Malware
items.summary: Adversaries often try to install malware to disrupt or control a device; devices lacking anti-malware software are more vulnerable to this attack.

slides.script: Second grid, EK 4.1.C.5 through C.7 — the network-and-defense weaknesses. Open ports, C.5: an open port is simply a way in, and adversaries leverage it to connect to the device. Missing or misconfigured firewall, C.6: adversaries send crafted malicious data to disrupt or take over a device, and with no firewall there's nothing to filter that data out. Missing anti-malware, C.7: adversaries try to install malware, and a device without anti-malware is far more exposed to that. Tie each to its EK. On the farm, that grain sensor with an open port and no firewall is a textbook C.5 and C.6 target.

slides.label: Assessing and Documenting Risk
slides.script: Section four. Learning objective 4.1.D. Naming a weakness isn't enough — you have to rate the risk and write it down. The College Board gives three levels, high, moderate, and low, keyed to how critical the device is and how bad exploitation would be. Ten minutes, and this is exactly what scenario 4A asks you to produce.

slides.heading: Rating Device-Vulnerability Risk: High, Moderate, Low
slides.context: Risk from device vulnerabilities depends on the device's criticality and the data or services it provides (EK 4.1.D.1); the level is high, moderate, or low (EK 4.1.D.2–D.4).
rows: High (EK 4.1.D.2)
Potentially compromises sensitive data or critical operations
An organization hasn't installed the latest update for its email server, which patched a known critical vulnerability
Moderate (EK 4.1.D.3)
Arises from weak authentication, or vulnerabilities less likely to be exploited
Water-treatment pump controllers reachable by username and password remotely, with no MFA
Low (EK 4.1.D.4)
Vulnerabilities that, if exploited, would have little impact
An employee's laptop has telnet port 23 open
slides.note: AP TIP: The same vulnerability can sit at different levels depending on the DEVICE. Unpatched software on a critical email server is high; the impact and the device's criticality set the level (EK 4.1.D.1).
slides.script: Three risk levels, straight from EK 4.1.D.2 through D.4, and EK 4.1.D.1 tells you the level depends on how critical the device is and the data or services it provides. High risk: exploitation could compromise sensitive data or critical operations — the CED's example is an unpatched email server with a known critical vulnerability. Moderate: it stems from weak authentication, or a vulnerability less likely to be exploited — the water-treatment pumps reachable by password with no MFA. Low: little real impact if exploited — a laptop with telnet port 23 open. The crucial point: the same weakness can sit at different levels depending on the device's criticality. This table is the engine of scenario 4A.

slides.heading: What Goes in the Risk Documentation
bullets: Risk from device vulnerabilities can let an adversary impersonate an authorized user, remotely control a device, encrypt a drive to ransom the data, or wipe memory — destroying data or rendering the device inoperable (EK 4.1.D.1).
The level of risk varies with the criticality of the device and the services it provides or the data it stores (EK 4.1.D.1).
Document for each risk: the vulnerable device, the vulnerability, the malware or technique an adversary would use, the likely impact, and the assigned level — high, moderate, or low (EK 4.1.D.2–D.4).
In scenario 4A you do exactly this for the connected farm: list each device's vulnerability and rate it, then recommend controls.
slides.script: Documenting risk is the deliverable. EK 4.1.D.1 names what's at stake — an adversary could impersonate a user, remotely control the device, ransom the drive, or wipe its memory and render it inoperable — and the level of risk varies with how critical the device is and what data or services it holds. So for each risk you write down the vulnerable device, the specific vulnerability, the malware or technique that would exploit it, the likely impact, and your assigned level: high, moderate, or low. That is precisely the report scenario 4A asks for on the connected farm — vulnerability, rating, recommendation, for every device.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Independent Writing
slides.subheading: Both questions map to LO 4.1.C and 4.1.D. Complete on your Guided Notes packet.
prompts: A connected grain sensor has no firewall, so an adversary sends crafted malicious data straight to it and partially takes it over. Name the exact CED vulnerability and cite its EK. Then explain why a missing firewall (C.6) is a different weakness from an open port (C.5).

The farm's email server is missing a patch for a known critical vulnerability that could expose sensitive customer records. Assign a CED risk level — high, moderate, or low — cite the EK, and write the one-line risk-documentation entry you'd record for it.
slides.directions: Independent. 6 minutes. Full sentences. I will collect Guided Notes at the end of class.
slides.script: Two questions, six minutes, independent, full sentences. Question one: the grain sensor with no firewall taking in malicious data is EK 4.1.C.6, a missing firewall that can't filter the data — and you should distinguish that from an open port, C.5, which is about reaching the device in the first place. Question two: the unpatched email server with a known critical vulnerability exposing customer records is high risk, EK 4.1.D.2, because it threatens sensitive data on a critical device — then write the documentation line: device, vulnerability, technique, impact, level. This is the scenario-4A skill. Go.

slides.heading: Device Vulnerabilities, in One Slide
points: Five device types by purpose: server serves others, personal is one-person, handheld is small and battery-powered, embedded is part of a machine, IoT is an internet-connected embedded device (EK 4.1.A.1–A.5).

Nine malware types by behavior: virus (user runs it), worm (spreads alone), trojan/RAT (hidden / remote control), ransomware (encrypt + demand), spyware (watches), keylogger (keystrokes), logic bomb (conditions), rootkit (hides in OS), fileless (lives in RAM) (EK 4.1.B.1–B.3).
Seven exploited weaknesses: unpatched software, weak authentication, missing BIOS/UEFI password, autorun, open ports, missing firewall, missing anti-malware (EK 4.1.C.1–C.7).
Three risk levels by criticality and impact: high (sensitive data / critical ops), moderate (weak auth / less likely), low (little impact) — documented per device (EK 4.1.D.1–D.4).
slides.next: Apply all four objectives to the connected-farm scenario 4A — tag each device, predict its malware and weaknesses, and assign and document a risk level. Then study for the Lesson 4.1 quiz, which spans 4.1.A through D.
slides.script: Everything in one slide. Five device categories defined by purpose. Nine malware types defined by behavior, with the virus-versus-worm line front and center. Seven device weaknesses adversaries exploit, from unpatched software to missing anti-malware. And three risk levels — high, moderate, low — set by the device's criticality and the impact of exploitation, then written into documentation. Next you take all four to scenario 4A: tag every device on the connected farm, predict the malware and weaknesses it faces, and assign and document a risk level. Review your Guided Notes — be able to match any behavior or scenario to its EK cold, because that's exactly how the Lesson 4.1 quiz is built.

guide.title: Lesson 4.1 — Device Vulnerabilities — Teacher Guide
guide.subtitle: Unit 4 · Topic 4.1 · 6 class periods · Anchor: Designing Secure Connected Farm Equipment
overview: Lesson 4.1, Device Vulnerabilities, opens Unit 4 (Securing Devices) by naming the categories of computing devices, identifying the kinds of malware adversaries deploy, and explaining how adversaries exploit common device weaknesses to cause loss, damage, disruption, or destruction. It is the threat-side foundation for the device-hardening controls that the rest of the unit builds, paired with the unit scenario 4A, Designing Secure Connected Farm Equipment, in which students research how an adversary might exploit internet-connected farm equipment and assess the resulting risk.

This Day 1 file carries the lesson-level quiz and this teacher guide, which span three learning objectives in the topic's tested scope: identifying types of computing devices (4.1.A) — server, personal, handheld, embedded, and IoT; identifying the malware used in a cyberattack (4.1.B) — viruses, worms, trojans and RATs, ransomware, spyware, keyloggers, logic bombs, rootkits, and fileless malware; and explaining how adversaries exploit common device vulnerabilities (4.1.C) — unpatched software, weak authentication, missing BIOS/UEFI passwords, autorun, open ports, missing firewalls, and missing anti-malware software.
los: LO 4.1.A — Identify types of computing devices.
LO 4.1.B — Identify the type of malware used in a cyberattack.
LO 4.1.C — Explain how adversaries can exploit common device vulnerabilities to cause loss, damage, disruption, or destruction.
LO 4.1.D — Assess and document risks from device vulnerabilities.
eks: EK 4.1.A.1 — Server computers are devices that provide one or more services to other computers (e.g., DNS, DHCP, FTP). Any computer can be a server, and in an enterprise environment servers typically have more processing power and storage than a personal computer.

EK 4.1.A.2 — Personal computers are devices that are designed to be used by one person for work or recreational purposes (e.g., word processing, graphic design, web browsing, and media production or viewing). These include desktop, laptop, and notebook computers.

EK 4.1.A.3 — Handheld computers (also called mobile computers or information appliances) are smaller than personal computers and run on battery power. These include tablets, smartphones, and wearable technology like smart watches.

EK 4.1.A.4 — Embedded computers are devices that are part of a machine. Embedded devices have specific instruction sets for interfacing with the specialized components of the machine they're embedded in. Embedded computers tend to be slower and cheaper than other computers and have minimal storage.

EK 4.1.A.5 — Everyday devices with embedded computers are often called Internet of Things (IoT) devices. Embedded computers are found in transportation (e.g., cars, trains, and airplanes), devices that operate critical infrastructure (e.g., operating circuit breakers at electrical substations and pumps at water treatment plants), medical equipment (e.g., IV pumps, MRI scanners, pacemakers, and insulin pumps), and everyday devices like washing machines, coffee makers, and thermostats.
EK 4.1.B.1 — Malware is malicious software that can damage or destroy a device or network, or allow an adversary access to a device and the data on the device.

EK 4.1.B.2 — Malware is often used as a tool to accomplish part of an adversary's plan to achieve their ultimate goal(s). There are many types of malware, such as: viruses are malware that must be activated by a user executing or opening a file; worms spread from one computer to another without human interaction; trojans are malware embedded in other software that seems harmless, and remote access trojans (RATs) provide an adversary with remote access to the target system; ransomware encrypts a device's files, preventing the user from accessing files and typically demanding payment for a decryption key within a fixed amount of time; spyware tracks a user's actions on a computer and sends information back to an adversary; a keylogger is software or hardware that logs the user's keystrokes and sends the information back to the adversary, from which adversaries can often extract usernames and passwords; logic bombs are set to trigger their effect only when a specific set of conditions are met; and a rootkit is sophisticated malware that gets into the target computer's operating system and can control nearly every aspect of the system, including making the rootkit itself invisible to detection.
EK 4.1.B.3 — While most malware is a file or a collection of files, fileless malware is malicious code that lives in RAM and uses legitimate programs already installed on a device to compromise it.

EK 4.1.C.1 — Adversaries can develop exploits for known vulnerabilities in software (including operating systems). Devices with unpatched software are vulnerable to these exploits, which could allow an adversary to crash a system, view user actions, enable or disable various services or components on the device (e.g., turning on a webcam or microphone), or even take control of the device to issue their own commands including commands to steal or destroy information on the device.
EK 4.1.C.2 — Adversaries can take advantage of weak authentication requirements by guessing a user's password or using social engineering to get a user to divulge their password.

EK 4.1.C.3 — When systems don't have a password on the basic input output system (BIOS) or unified extensible firmware interface (UEFI), an adversary can boot a computer into a special mode (e.g., "recovery mode") that gives them higher-level privileges. Without BIOS or UEFI protection, adversaries can load their own operating system onto a device from an external drive and use specialized tools to alter or create user profiles, including changing user passwords.
EK 4.1.C.4 — Adversaries can load malware onto an external drive, and if autorun is enabled, then a device will run the malware when the external drive is inserted.
EK 4.1.C.5 — Adversaries can leverage open ports to connect to a device.

EK 4.1.C.6 — Adversaries can send malicious data to devices to disrupt them or attempt to take control of them. Devices that have no firewall (or a misconfigured firewall) cannot filter out this malicious data.
EK 4.1.C.7 — Adversaries often attempt to install malware on a device to disrupt or control it. Devices lacking anti-malware software are more vulnerable to this type of attack.
materials: Projector / slide deck (teacher · deep-dive version) for each of the six days.

Printed/Digital Day 1–6 Guided Notes (student copies). Workflow: students fill the notes in live during the slide presentation; anything missed can be completed — or checked — afterward against the lesson page, which carries all the content. The formal answer KEYs stay in this folder and are never posted to the site.
Printed Lesson 4.1 quiz (student copies) for the final day of the topic.

Unit scenario 4A, Designing Secure Connected Farm Equipment — students research how an adversary might take advantage of internet-connected farm equipment, assess the risk from possible vulnerabilities, and produce a report with recommendations.
prereqs: Unit 1 vocabulary of confidentiality, integrity, and availability, and the Unit 2 ideas of adversaries, attack phases, and security controls. Learners should be comfortable with the basic terms operating system, port, firewall, and authentication before naming device-level threats against them.

pacing.focus: Types of computing devices: server, personal, and handheld (LO 4.1.A)

segments.segment: QOTD (U4/L4.1/Q1) + bell ringer (which devices run the connected farm?)
Server computers and what makes a device a server (EK 4.1.A.1)
Personal and handheld computers — desktops, laptops, tablets, smartphones, wearables (EK 4.1.A.2–A.3)

pacing.focus: Embedded computers and the Internet of Things (LO 4.1.A)

segments.segment: Embedded computers: part of a machine, minimal storage, specialized instruction sets (EK 4.1.A.4)
IoT devices in transportation, critical infrastructure, medical equipment, and everyday devices (EK 4.1.A.5)

pacing.focus: Malware definition and the major file-based types (LO 4.1.B)

segments.segment: What malware is and how adversaries use it as a tool (EK 4.1.B.1–B.2)
Viruses, worms, trojans/RATs, and ransomware compared (EK 4.1.B.2)

pacing.focus: Spyware, keyloggers, logic bombs, rootkits, and fileless malware (LO 4.1.B)

segments.segment: Spyware, keyloggers, and logic bombs — surveillance and conditional triggers (EK 4.1.B.2)
Rootkits hiding in the OS and fileless malware living in RAM (EK 4.1.B.2–B.3)

pacing.focus: Exploiting software, authentication, and boot/firmware weaknesses (LO 4.1.C)

segments.segment: Weak authentication: guessing and social engineering (EK 4.1.C.2)
Missing BIOS/UEFI passwords, recovery mode, and autorun on external drives (EK 4.1.C.3–C.4)

pacing.focus: Exploiting open ports, missing firewalls, and missing anti-malware (LO 4.1.C)

segments.segment: Open ports and connecting to a device; malicious data and missing firewalls (EK 4.1.C.5–C.6)

misconceptions.heading: Viruses vs. worms
misconceptions.myth: Viruses and worms are interchangeable terms for self-spreading malware.
misconceptions.reality: EK 4.1.B.2 distinguishes them: a virus must be activated by a user executing or opening a file, while a worm spreads from one computer to another without human interaction. The defining difference is whether human action is required to spread.

misconceptions.heading: Fileless malware still leaves a file to scan
misconceptions.myth: All malware is a file on disk, so anti-malware can always find it by scanning the drive.
misconceptions.reality: EK 4.1.B.3 states that fileless malware is malicious code that lives in RAM and uses legitimate programs already installed on a device, so it is not a file sitting on the drive waiting to be scanned like most malware is.

misconceptions.heading: Any computer can be a server
misconceptions.myth: A server is a special class of expensive hardware that is fundamentally different from a personal computer.
misconceptions.reality: EK 4.1.A.1 states that any computer can be a server — a server is a device that provides one or more services to other computers (e.g., DNS, DHCP, FTP). In an enterprise environment servers typically have more processing power and storage, but that is a tendency, not what makes a device a server.

support: Give students a one-page chart mapping each device category (server, personal, handheld, embedded, IoT) to a defining trait and an example from the CED, so they can sort new devices by what the device is for rather than by its size.
Build a malware matching grid with two columns — the malware name and its one-line behavior from EK 4.1.B.2 — and have students complete it before attempting any scenario item.
extension: Have students take the scenario-4A connected farm equipment and tag each device as server, personal, handheld, embedded, or IoT, then identify which 4.1.C vulnerability each is most exposed to.

Ask students to compare unpatched software (EK 4.1.C.1) with missing anti-malware software (EK 4.1.C.7) and explain why patching closes a specific known hole while anti-malware addresses installed malware more broadly.

guide.answerKeyNote: Each question's full rationale is on the KEY copy of the quiz (print it with the answer-key option). The short answer key below is pulled straight from the quiz on this page, so it always matches the questions students see.

quiz.title: Lesson 4.1 — Device Vulnerabilities — Quiz
quiz.instructions: Choose the single best answer. 11 questions, 20 minutes, no notes.

questions.stem: On the farm network, one machine handles DNS and DHCP requests for every other computer and is built with far more processing power and storage than the staff laptops. A new technician asks what category this machine belongs to. Which type of computing device is it?
options: A server computer — a device that provides one or more services to other computers
A handheld computer — a battery-powered device smaller than a personal computer
An embedded computer — a device that is part of a machine with minimal storage
A personal computer — a device designed to be used by one person for work or recreation
questions.rationale: EK 4.1.A.1 defines a server as a device that provides one or more services to other computers (e.g., DNS, DHCP, FTP), typically with more processing power and storage in an enterprise environment. Option B (handheld, EK 4.1.A.3) is battery-powered and smaller. Option C (embedded, EK 4.1.A.4) is part of a machine with minimal storage. Option D (personal, EK 4.1.A.2) is built for one person, not for providing services to other computers.

questions.stem: A field worker monitors irrigation from a battery-powered smartphone and a smart watch while away from any desk. Both devices are smaller than a laptop and run on battery power. According to the CED, what category of computing device are these?
options: Server computers — devices that provide services such as DNS or DHCP to others
Handheld computers — battery-powered devices smaller than personal computers, including smartphones and wearables
Embedded computers — devices that are part of a machine with specialized instruction sets
Personal computers — desktop, laptop, and notebook computers used by one person
questions.rationale: EK 4.1.A.3 defines handheld computers (also called mobile computers) as smaller than personal computers and battery-powered, including tablets, smartphones, and wearable technology like smart watches. Option A (server, EK 4.1.A.1) provides services to other computers. Option C (embedded, EK 4.1.A.4) is part of a machine. Option D (personal, EK 4.1.A.2) names desktops, laptops, and notebooks — not smartphones and wearables.

questions.stem: A connected tractor contains a small, low-cost computer that is part of the machine itself. It has minimal storage and a specialized instruction set for interfacing with the tractor's components, and because it connects to the internet it is described as part of the Internet of Things. Which two CED terms best describe this computer?
options: A personal computer that is also a server — a device providing services to the farm
A handheld computer that is also a server — a battery-powered device offering services
An embedded computer that is also an IoT device — part of a machine and internet-connected
A server computer that is also a handheld device — a small unit with minimal storage
questions.rationale: EK 4.1.A.4 defines embedded computers as part of a machine, with specialized instruction sets and minimal storage, and EK 4.1.A.5 notes that everyday internet-connected devices with embedded computers are often called IoT devices. Options A, B, and D each pair the device with a category it does not fit — a server provides services to other computers, a handheld is a standalone battery-powered device, and minimal storage does not make a device handheld.

questions.stem: An adversary emails a farm employee an attachment that looks like an invoice. Nothing happens until the employee opens the file, which then activates malicious code that corrupts records on that computer. Which type of malware is described?
options: A worm — it spreads from one computer to another without any human interaction
A virus — it must be activated by a user executing or opening a file
Spyware — it tracks a user's actions and sends the information back to an adversary
A logic bomb — it triggers only when a specific set of conditions is met
questions.rationale: EK 4.1.B.2 defines a virus as malware that must be activated by a user executing or opening a file, which matches the employee opening the attachment. A worm (option A) spreads without human interaction. Spyware (option C) tracks user actions. A logic bomb (option D) triggers on specific conditions such as a date, not on a user opening a file.

questions.stem: Malware on the farm's record-keeping computer encrypts all of its files, locks the user out, and displays a screen demanding payment within a fixed time in exchange for a decryption key. Which type of malware is this?
options: A rootkit — it embeds in the operating system and hides itself from detection
A keylogger — it logs keystrokes and sends usernames and passwords to the adversary
Ransomware — it encrypts files and demands payment for a decryption key within a fixed time
A trojan — it is embedded in other software that seems harmless to gain access
questions.rationale: EK 4.1.B.2 defines ransomware as malware that encrypts a device's files, prevents access, and presents a screen demanding payment for a decryption key within a fixed amount of time — exactly the described behavior. A rootkit (option A) hides in the OS. A keylogger (option B) captures keystrokes. A trojan (option D) hides in seemingly harmless software.

questions.stem: A malware analyst reviews three samples. I. Code that lives in RAM and uses legitimate programs already installed on the device, rather than existing as a file on disk. II. Sophisticated malware that gets into the operating system, can control nearly every aspect of it, and makes itself invisible to detection. III. Malware that spreads from one computer to another with no human interaction. Match each sample to the correct term.
options: I = fileless malware, II = rootkit, III = worm
I = worm, II = fileless malware, III = rootkit
I = rootkit, II = worm, III = fileless malware
I = fileless malware, II = worm, III = rootkit
questions.rationale: EK 4.1.B.3 defines fileless malware as code that lives in RAM and uses legitimate installed programs — that is I. EK 4.1.B.2 defines a rootkit as malware that gets into the OS, controls nearly every aspect of it, and hides itself — that is II — and a worm as malware that spreads without human interaction — that is III. Only option A maps all three correctly; the others swap at least two terms.

questions.stem: The farm's email server is missing the most recent update, which included a patch for a known critical vulnerability. An adversary develops an exploit for that vulnerability, crashes services, and ultimately issues commands to steal data from the server. Which vulnerability did the adversary exploit?
options: Autorun was enabled, so inserting an external drive automatically ran the adversary's malware
The device ran unpatched software with a known vulnerability for which an exploit was developed
There was no firewall, so the device could not filter out malicious data sent to it
Weak authentication let the adversary guess the password or socially engineer the user
questions.rationale: EK 4.1.C.1 states adversaries can develop exploits for known vulnerabilities in software and that devices with unpatched software are vulnerable, allowing an adversary to crash a system or take control to steal or destroy information — matching the unpatched email server. Option A is autorun (EK 4.1.C.4), option C is a missing firewall (EK 4.1.C.6), and option D is weak authentication (EK 4.1.C.2), none of which involve a missing software patch.

questions.stem: A laptop in the farm office has no password set on its BIOS/UEFI. An adversary with brief physical access boots it into a special recovery mode that grants higher-level privileges, then loads their own operating system from an external drive to change a user's password. Which vulnerability made this possible?
options: Anti-malware software was missing, so installed malware could disrupt or control the device
An open port let the adversary connect to the device over the network
Malicious data reached the device unfiltered — there was no firewall to block it
There was no BIOS/UEFI password, allowing a boot into recovery mode with higher privileges
questions.rationale: EK 4.1.C.3 states that without a BIOS or UEFI password an adversary can boot into a special mode such as recovery mode for higher-level privileges, load their own operating system from an external drive, and alter user profiles including passwords. Option A is missing anti-malware (EK 4.1.C.7), option B is an open port (EK 4.1.C.5), and option C is a missing firewall (EK 4.1.C.6) — none describe an unprotected boot firmware.

questions.stem: A connected grain sensor has no firewall. An adversary sends crafted malicious data directly to the device, and with nothing to filter the incoming data the device is disrupted and partially taken over. Which CED vulnerability does this illustrate?
options: A device with autorun enabled runs malware as soon as an external drive is inserted
A device with unpatched software is open to exploits for known vulnerabilities
A device with weak authentication lets an adversary guess or socially engineer the password
A device with no firewall cannot filter out malicious data sent to disrupt or control it
questions.rationale: EK 4.1.C.6 states that adversaries can send malicious data to devices to disrupt or take control of them, and that devices with no firewall (or a misconfigured one) cannot filter out this malicious data. Option B is autorun (EK 4.1.C.4), option C is unpatched software (EK 4.1.C.1), and option D is weak authentication (EK 4.1.C.2) — none concern filtering malicious data at the device.

questions.stem: A company has not installed the latest update for its email server, which patched a known critical vulnerability that could expose sensitive customer records. Using the CED risk levels, this is BEST classified as:
options: High risk — it could compromise sensitive data or critical operations
Moderate risk — the vulnerability is unlikely to ever be exploited
Low risk — exploitation here would have little real impact
No risk — a missing software update is not a device vulnerability
questions.rationale: High risks from device vulnerabilities involve potentially compromising sensitive data or critical operations; an unpatched critical email-server vulnerability exposing customer records fits this level (EK 4.1.D.2). Options B and C understate the impact, and D is wrong since unpatched software is itself a device vulnerability.

questions.stem: A water-treatment plant's embedded pump controllers can be reached remotely with only a username and password — no multi-factor authentication. Exploitation is possible but constrained, and the impact would be contained. This BEST illustrates which risk level?
options: High risk — the stored data is certain to be destroyed at once
No risk — password authentication removes the threat entirely
Moderate risk — it stems from weak authentication requirements
Low risk — exploitation here would have little or no impact
questions.rationale: Moderate risks can arise from weak authentication requirements, such as remote access without MFA — the CED's water-treatment example (EK 4.1.D.3). Option A overstates certainty, B is false since weak authentication does not remove the threat, and D describes the low-risk level (EK 4.1.D.4).
